title: Transformers, the tech behind LLMs
description: A visual walkthrough of how transformer models turn text into predictions through embeddings, attention, and MLP layers.

narration.text: Large language models like GPT are built on an architecture called the transformer. Let's open up the whole machine and see how text flows through it to predict the next word.
It all starts at the input. Each token, roughly a word or piece of a word, becomes a long list of numbers called an embedding, a vector living in a high dimensional space.
Directions in this space carry meaning. Words with similar meanings sit near each other, and as the vector moves through the network it soaks up context from its neighbors.
These vectors then pass through a stack of identical blocks. GPT-2 has twelve of them, and each block has two main parts: an attention step and a multilayer perceptron.
Let's zoom into a single block. Information enters, gets refined, and is added back into what's called the residual stream, the running tally of each token's meaning.
The first stage is attention. This is where tokens look at each other and pass information around, so each word can update its meaning based on context.
Each arc shows how strongly one token attends to another. A word like sat might pull information from cat to figure out who is doing the sitting.
Attention runs in parallel across many heads. GPT-2 has twelve per block, and each head can specialize, tracking grammar, references, or other relationships.
After tokens have shared information, each vector passes through the multilayer perceptron. Here every token is processed independently, where much of the model's stored knowledge lives.
This pattern repeats, attention then MLP, block after block. With each layer the representations grow richer, building up an increasingly contextual picture of the text.
Let's actually run it. We feed in the phrase the cat sat on the, and watch the activations and attention light up as the model processes it.
At the very end, the final token's vector is mapped to a score for every possible next token. A softmax turns those scores into probabilities.
The model predicts mat as a likely continuation. By sampling a token, appending it, and running again, the transformer generates text one word at a time.

So a transformer is really just embeddings flowing through repeated attention and MLP layers, gradually transforming meaning until it can guess what comes next. That simple recipe, scaled up enormously, is what powers modern language models.

affordances.description: Move the camera to frame a part of the model.

args.target: one of: overview, embeddings, block, attention, mlp, head, unembed, token
args.index: block index (0-based) when target is block/attention/mlp/head
args.head: attention head index when target is head
args.tokenIndex: token index when target is token

affordances.description: Highlight a transformer block by index.
Highlight and focus a specific attention head in a block.
Draw the attention pattern (token-to-token arcs) for a block. Omit head to average over all heads.
Show residual-stream activations at a block as a heatmap; mode 'values' also prints numbers.
Set decimal places shown for numeric values.
Run the real model forward pass on input text and light up activations/attention.

args.text: input text to feed the model

affordances.description: Show a short caption/label on screen.
